subject: Chemistry
chapter_name: Periodic Table and Periodicity of Properties

note_data.title: Periodic Table and Periodicity of Properties
keyPoints: Periodic Table — A table in which elements are arranged in order of increasing atomic number
Modern Periodic Law — Physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers
Period — A horizontal row in the periodic table containing elements with same number of electron shells
Group/Family — A vertical column in periodic table containing elements with same number of valence electrons
Alkali metals (Group IA) are most reactive metals, stored under kerosene to prevent reaction with air
Noble gases (Group VIIIA) are chemically inert due to complete outer electron shells
Atomic radius decreases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge
Ionization energy increases across a period and decreases down a group
Electronegativity increases across a period and decreases down a group
Metallic character decreases across a period and increases down a group

definitions.definition: A table in which elements are arranged in order of increasing atomic number
A horizontal row in the periodic table containing elements with same number of electron shells
A vertical column in periodic table containing elements with same number of valence electrons
The minimum energy required to remove the most loosely bound electron from an isolated gaseous atom
The ability of an atom to attract shared pair of electrons in a covalent bond

importantQuestions.question: Write down the electronic configuration of sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl).
importantQuestions.answer:
Na (11): 2, 8, 1
Cl (17): 2, 8, 7
Sodium has 1 valence electron, Chlorine has 7 valence electrons.

importantQuestions.question: Explain the trend of atomic radius in periodic table with examples.
importantQuestions.answer:
Atomic radius decreases across a period: Nuclear charge increases while electron shells remain same, pulling electrons closer. Example: Na > Mg > Al > Si.
Atomic radius increases down a group: New electron shells are added, increasing distance from nucleus. Example: Li < Na < K < Rb.
This trend helps predict chemical properties and bonding behavior of elements.

mcqs.question: Which element has the highest electronegativity?
Elements in the same group have the same:

commonMistakes: Students confuse periods with groups - remember periods are horizontal rows
Mixing up trends - atomic radius decreases across period but ionization energy increases
Writing wrong electronic configurations - always count electrons equal to atomic number
Forgetting that noble gases are inert, not reactive
Confusing valence electrons with total electrons in an atom
examTips: This chapter usually has 2-3 MCQs and 1 long question in BSEK papers
Memorize electronic configurations of first 20 elements - frequently tested
Practice drawing periodic table trends - appears every year
Learn properties of specific groups (alkali metals, halogens, noble gases)
Remember exact definitions - BSEK marks are deducted for incorrect wording
formulas: Valence electrons = Group number (for main groups)
note_data.keyDiagram: Modern Periodic Table showing 18 groups and 7 periods, with s, p, d, f blocks clearly marked. Must show increasing atomic number from left to right, and indicate trends of atomic radius, ionization energy, and electronegativity with arrows.
